metadata.title: DISCO + QA Wolf - AI Testing Partnership

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: him and I talked about our traditional model at qawolf which is services and a platform. But then we're also starting to offer our platform which our in house engineers use. So I guess this conversation can go either ways. But he basically said that it seems like you wanted your teams to continue working with a tool like the developers themselves, creating the tests and executing the tests. So, long story short that's a long winded answer to say that I want to hear what your goals are with testing your current concerns and then work towards just understanding, I guess what you'd want to see from us. And then we can also do an AI platform demo for you today as well if you want to see that. Sure that'd be.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: Awesome.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: I'm just curious first off, where are you based at? I'm just curious.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: I'm personally based out of Austin. I was at the home office before we decided to do remote stuff.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Nice. Yeah, we're remote first as well. I'm in Boston, Ian's in Buffalo, New York, and we're headquartered in Seattle. We have people all over the place. Yeah. I also noticed you've been at disco for like, do you guys pronounce it disco or do you say like DISC o nope.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: It's disco.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Okay. I had to make sure because I just questioned that too before. I was like, it's all caps and I was like, maybe that means I'm saying it wrong, but seems like you've been there a while and like worked your way up to engineering manager. I guess, have you seen like a ton of growth at disco over the last like eight years or?

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: Yeah, absolutely. The company's very different from the company that I joined eight years ago.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Yeah. Is it through a lot of acquisitions the growth came from or in house development?

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: No, it's almost entirely in house. So we've bought one product but it is also a product that most of our competitors give away for free. So we have it just so that we can check the box for our clients and say that, yeah, we can do that.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Classic. Yeah, classic. That's so funny. Yeah, I think a lot of people do that these days, I mean, or offer open source offerings to try to get people in the door as well. I kind of like some other options like that. But cool. Cool. I guess like overall just from your viewpoint, going into 20 26 was curious like what your like top two three goals. This doesn't have to be pertaining necessarily to QA. Like just, I guess honestly interested to hear where QA stacks up with your overall goals going into this year. Yeah.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: Let me give you an even broader picture. I'll tell you kind of what our QA story is at the moment and then where we're trying to go and then why we're trying to go there. So right now, we have a lot of cypress tests and that's our end to end test. And the reason that we ended up there is because we had a QA department and the QA department was almost a full release behind in testing the software that was going out. So it effectively was we were testing in production on customers that was terrible. We tried to get them to work ahead and work with engineering, but they just weren't able to keep up with it. And the engineers weren't wanting to communicate. So we stopped that experiment. And now the front end engineers are responsible for writing the end to end tests along with the features. And so we'll groom that out as part of our big initiatives and epics and everything. And it's just work that they take on… what we're seeing right now is we have really decent coverage of our happy paths. But that doesn't give us a lot of confidence with the new AI tools that have started to take over. We're starting to see huge volumes of code come through and we're bottlenecking in places that we never bottlenecked before like code reviews. And so we're looking for ways to have higher confidence with lower oversight. In the past, we had very high trust factor because we knew all of our engineers were really good people and they knew they wanted what was best for the company. And so you could trust without a lot of verification that their code was going to be good now with it being largely AI driven, you have no trust, you have no idea if what the code's coming in is secure if it's performant. So, a lot of our reviewers are spending extra time there on top of having so much more code to review. So the second thing that we're trying to achieve this year beyond just that is we're trying to get coverage numbers. We want to start to be able to see what coverage do we have on our happy paths today? That's manually curated. We just have somebody look at the test scenarios and they're like, well, we don't cover that so that's minus one. And they do cover this. So that's plus one. And we get this like really kludgy number that's out of date as soon as you're done with it. Yeah. So we want to have higher confidence and overall, it would be nice to have a score with how much we have as far as coverage, obviously, we have unit test coverage, but that almost doesn't count because in an end to end systems world with microservices, unless you've tested all of your microservices together, you really haven't tested any of them, right?

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Right. So essentially like end to end tests and then do some like integration testing, have confidence in all of your releases and just, yeah, I guess like new features functionality especially like the AI or with the help of AI coding that you're doing right now and then just increase coverage across the board. It's basically overall priority.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: We're trying to push left, you know, I'm sure everybody is.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Yeah, essentially shift left. Yeah, that makes sense. And so on the confidence with releases side, I mean, one thing Jamie mentioned was that the like I guess way your business is run or just maybe just because you're in like the legal business that any bug could cause for like an entire like partner to like leave you or like just an entire like thing like, so have you seen bugs become an issue? And like that's kind of like where the confidence and releases are coming from or?

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: They haven't and that's why we have the bottlenecks forming in the places that they are, because we have such thorough human review. All of our code in our deployment process will get cut a week before we plan to do a release. And we have a ton of automated testing that will run for the full week and it runs every day, the same test over and over again. Then we have human validation that runs two to three times a week and they'll go through and run very common scenarios, happy paths, all that kind of stuff.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Interesting. And so how often are you releasing right now? Is it like by every week? Every week? Okay. So then the process is you?

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: I guess.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Sorry, how much manual testing is being done? Like when you say coverage numbers? Like what percentage is automated right now on?

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: That we… see those as very separate numbers. So our automated test coverage even if it was 100 percent, we would still do a lot of manual testing because we want the human factor of hey, all of a sudden, this thing that was on the left side is over here, whereas a robot's like found that QA element that's fine. It's.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: so true, it's funny that you actually understand this because a lot of people say we're going to get to 100 percent automation and then not have to think about manual again. But there's still a lot of manual. Especially when you shift left, it's like new feature testing. Like anything like that will still be manually tested. And then you have your like core regression suites run through the automation and then kind of like from there just switch to production at the end. So yeah, I guess like overall. So it seems that you have no bug issues. Is there a goal to increase that release velocity at all? Like from a week to faster? Or is that not necessarily in the preview… we would.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: love that, but that's not being held back by testing right now. The reason that we have a weekly deployment cadence is because we have an old C sharp app that has an extremely manual release process. It takes an entire day for them to release it. The UI is along for the ride. It takes us like three button clicks and we're out, but we are waiting on them that's why we have a delayed cadence. Interesting.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Okay. And then I know that to use of the front end engineers are doing the UI testing like dudes, I mean you have like back end engineers doing API testing or are they the front end engineers doing everything? We?

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: Have a lot of different layers to our testing. So everybody doesn't does unit tests. The integration tests are done by the system owners. In most cases, the UI will do integrations with like our graph. And then things going into the graph will have their integration tests or between key services, they'll do integration testing. And then on top of all that, we have our end to end tests for our scenario, it's very expensive for us to run these because we have to spin up a whole data. Well, more than one, we spin up probably 50 databases for every one thing we're going to test. And then it has to run through this ingest process. Our setup on a lightning fast cadence takes 40 minutes for a new thing to be spun up so that it can be tested. And then the tests themselves. They're taking about 12 hours right now to get all the way through all of them. And that's because of limitations in our auth provider, we can't go parallel more than four instances at a time or we overwhelm our auth system.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: So that's like a it's not like a cypress sort of issue. It's like you're no matter what you'll never go above four parallel executions on your system.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: Yeah. What ends up happening is all of a sudden, all of our API calls start five hundreding, and then everything has to shut down and cool off for a second while auth catches up. And then we start over.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Interesting. I guess on a weekly cadence like 12 hours isn't I mean, that's a long time. But if there's no way to increase the parallel executions, like just increasing the automation and having more kind of like looped into that obviously would help in like the tail end of things. So, and you did say that the backend engineers are doing the API testing or is it the frontend engineers are doing end to end including API tests? Just curious there.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: So the way that we see this end to end exercises the UI, the exact same way that a user would. So we don't through those tests, validate API inputs or anything. There are lots of other types of integration tests that will validate to contract. But the end to end tests pretend they're just Joe schmo running around our app.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Got it. And that's how you want to continue operating. I.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: assume probably like we've never had a conversation about changing it. I don't know you could probably make a compelling case for it. Yeah.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Yeah. I would say a lot of people do the end to end including the API testing. I think that the same goes for integration testing. If you're testing an end to end test that goes in between two different platforms and the integrations there. It can kind of test the same thing as an integration test. So there's definitely ways not around it but just different ways of organizing everything here. Cool.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: I guess.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: The main priority is going to be increasing the coverage through or increasing your confidence through increasing coverage. And your engineers obviously own all that process. Now, do you imagine that that's where you want to continue? Is that those front end engineers continue to own all the testing in… the future? Well, I don't.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: feel strongly about it. It's convenient placing because right now we're resource heavy or we're resource constrained, I should say on the back end services, we've got a lot more back end work than front end if that were to flip over and suddenly we're resource constrained on the front end, it would probably be nice to be able to shift those around.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Right, right. And I know you're using cypress now. I think Jamie and I discussed you guys are looking into playwright. Is that true? So.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: We have both… primarily we steer people towards using cypress just because the dashboard's easier. And right now we're using their service that will rebalance our test runners. So, we know that when we've got, you know, 15 tests running, that each of the runners is doing something… we have playwright tests, we use those extensively when we need to inspect a payload for say a downloadable object or when we need to control browser uploads or we are even using them in our dls to do snapshot testing and things.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Interesting. I guess, do you know how many cypress tests you have versus playwright?

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: Well, as a percentage, it's probably like 80 20 cypress… to playwright. I don't know the exact number of tests off the top of my head though. Yeah, it would be in the tens of thousands, probably tens of.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Thousands for the cypress or for both.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: Yeah. For the cypress, we have 30 something suites that are all running with hundreds of tests.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Sweets with hundreds. So then in terms of where the coverage is lacking, I know you mentioned AI tooling that you're using for development, but is it spread evenly across these suites because of the processes that you're doing now for each of these? Or is it more like some are backlogged… with QA or something of that sort?

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: It's going to be the classic story of whatever is newest, has the most coverage. Whatever is oldest has the least.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Yeah, yeah, yeah.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: Which is unfortunate because that's where we need the most to have the most confidence in these refactors. So it ends up with, when we're going to go into old code, a lot of time is spent rewriting tests to make sure that we feel confident that we've got it covered before we start the new thing.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Yeah, no, and honestly, that's like pretty typical just that yeah, teams have like their old software and then they start adopting like new QA processes like midway through. And then it's impossible to catch up that like backlog essentially or.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: Test atrophy and you delete them. And suddenly your coverage went down and nobody notices. And then you have a weird dev who's like I hate this test deleted goes down again.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: It's so true.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.text: Yeah. I was gonna ask maybe why you have more recent release coverage and that kind of answers the question. Oh.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: That answer's really terrible because we have a lot of backbone code still. And the most recent runner we were using for unit testing was karma. And so when we switched over to jest, a lot of those tests, yeah. But jest not by anyway, that's a different story. We did that and we lost a bunch of test coverage because a lot of those tests required the whole app to be spinning up and jest doesn't run that way. And so we deleted a lot of the tests that required the full app to be running things that would validate. Like, I don't know the redux store was modified properly and stuff like that.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.text: I gotcha. Yeah, that makes sense.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Yeah. No, that is interesting. And then on the testing side of AI, just because I know Jamie and I discussed that your goals are kind of to incorporate more AI, I think to make your teams more efficient with testing at the very least. Do you have any AI testing features that you're using right now? And I don't know if cypress has much playwright maybe, but if.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: you consider using tools like cloud to write tests that's pretty common for us, you'll have people come in, committing different tests like unit level, some integration level with it. There have been usages of AI to refactor cypress tests, but I don't know if they're using it for whole creation steps, right?

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Right. They're using it for creation and then they're probably maintaining the scripts as they go from there, but the initial creation is going to be done through AI capabilities. Is that how I'm understanding it correctly?

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: I don't think we start with AI. I think we start with human written tests and then they're augmented or updated with AI things.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Right. Okay. No, that's interesting. Cool. And I think last thing here, maybe before getting into what qawolf is, and then we can show you a demo. Sorry… I guess looked into or thought about in terms of improving the process in the coming months or this year, what do you foresee as the perfect testing process in a few months from now? If?

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: You could, so I'm going to back out a little bit and share the overall goal. Testing is only one small piece in what we're trying to do. What we're trying to do is ship with high confidence and have high confidence in AI written code without high investment of human time in reviewing AI code. So the assertion right now is that by having greater amounts of automation in place, we can verify the outputs of code without having to read every line of that code. So that's what we're trying to do… testing is obviously a big part of it. Other AI skills are going to be a big part of it, but we don't have specific goals around what we're trying to do with say end to end tests.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: That makes sense. No, I appreciate that clarification I think on that front, then do you also see as getting time back for the engineers? Currently, the ones that are managing the test cases as a priority?

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: It's honestly not a big time spend for them right now. In the grand scheme of their day, the amount of time they spend writing tests is less than half a day a week.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Yeah. So about like 10 percent of their time spent on testing. And then you also already said that their front end or at least the front engineers have less on their plate. So it's like not like they're kind of like working overtime or anything of that sort to keep up with the tests. So then overall, the goal would be or maybe not overall but part of the goal that could be here is just like incorporating AI for the teams to be more efficient. One thing, just like from my top of mind, and like what I mentioned earlier is that we traditionally have a pairing of our platform with service offering. And Jamie did also mention that it's probably not a direction to look into, but just like for your kind of future knowledge. Especially as you mentioned if the workload flips in the future, we have like the engineers here like 120 plus QA engineers. And so if in the future, whether it be now or like later, they wanted to flip on some services like we would be able to step in and like obviously know the platform and like assist with taking like QA maintenance executions off your plate and so on. So, and creation, give.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: me a high level on that. Yeah, let me do that. Where do you integrate with companies when you're doing that? Yeah.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: So like just from the top, like we have our platform which we'll show you in a second. But the way it would be integrated in there is, I don't know if I asked him what cicd tools do you use?

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: It's you use Jenkins?

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: So what we could do is like integrate with Jenkins where a new bill, it's pushed through Jenkins. That could auto trigger. This is like one example, but this could auto trigger this test suite to run. And so maybe it's like whatever 200 tests for that specific application that we're testing. And then from there, once you get like the green light, which would essentially be like all the tests passed, you can also mark these tests by different like levels like to like blocking tests and like non blocking tests. But assuming all the blocking tests pass with green, you get like a green like check mark to go mark in your Jenkins. And then from there, you could push to production. And then if there are bugs, our team would then essentially integrate with your use Jira or like whatever ticketing system. Yes, we'd integrate with Jira and essentially push a bug report that shows like the steps where you produce the bug of video and then that would be sent through a ticket or through slack or teams, whichever is easier. Like be essentially we'd set up with you our entire engineering team or QA team. We set up into a slack channel or a teams channel with you and your developers. So then that's like where most of the communication would happen. Like the first part of what I explained is I guess like process fit in. And then I think like one last thing to point out is that the bug report side of it I guess is important because a lot of the times like flaky tests like aren't actually bugs. And so we're taking that work off your plate as well by doing the bug investigation and then taking that off your plate. So then you're really only like putting out the like real bugs and not create like fixing tests when they're just flaky test cases. So.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: At what point do you write the test? Yeah. So.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: The way like a partnership works, I think it's easier to start there is that we would get access to whatever application you care about. It could be all of them, could be one of them. And basically at your discretion, giving us access, they're giving us credentials. We would then scope out the like a test plan on that application or all of the applications. And then we could tell you, hey, this one, we could build 500 tests, but you could say, hey, we actually only care about 200 of these. Then we would then start the creation and get you to 50 percent coverage in three months and then 80 percent coverage in four months. Then we'd keep you above 80 percent like in our contracts above 80 percent automation of that test plan for the entire annual partnership. So how do.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: you handle like new feature ads?

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Yeah. So it can work with, I guess a couple of different ways. I would say the main way maybe would be like communicating in that slack. Do you use slack or teams? Just like that? Yeah, perfect slack's much better. So we would set up in the slack channel and you could have your developers all in there and message to our team and say, hey, we have this new feature. Maybe it's like a loom video like a video recording and you can send it in there in our platform. We have a coverage request area. We also would have like biweekly check ins. So maybe you just say like, hey, like communicate to your CSM and then maybe it's documented in excel or anything like that. So there's like a lot of flexibility on our team. We basically want to make it easy for you. So I'd say like the majority of it's just like plainly is just having open communication which our team will be there like every day to communicate. And then I think another caveat here is that everyone in your development org would have access to the platform. So if they wanted to go in, if you wanted to go in and look at like previous test runs or execute a test manually, and.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.text: Not have like the whole suite run.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Like anything like that. Then you have access to the platform too, but like the whole like majority of the work is just us taking it off your plate. Not like trying to make you like log into anything or it's really just going to be like seeing a lot of results and like reports in slack and then communication through slack as well. And then tickets through Jira basically.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.text: Okay. Interesting.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Yeah.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: I can see why Jamie would not want to do that. It doesn't sound like it would be a fast enough response time like you'd have to have, I don't know a six hour SLA for him to want to be willing to do that.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: So, on terms of like new test creation, you mean? Yeah.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: Like, yeah, I mean, I would notify you of things coming out to it's like testing.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: We do usually get back pretty quickly. I don't know if Ian, you can.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.text: Say like you have an idea on.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Like, I don't I wouldn't say like six hours if it's like at midnight type of situation, but if it's like in the morning like type of thing like by the end of the day would definitely be something that we would be able to do are?

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: You guys global or U. S based or we're global?

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Yeah, we have offices like U. S and then UK, and Australia. Okay. Interesting.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: Yeah. We're in the U. S, UK and India. Nice. I guess India's not too. Oh, I guess in Kyrgyzstan. Ah, yeah.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Yeah, yeah. But no, I guess India hours, can be all over the place. So, yeah, maybe it is 24 seven still… but I guess my point also explaining this is that we have opportunity to have some sort of like form of hybrid model here. And like that's why, like Jamie agreed, like to your point like that maybe like the platform is something that's worth exploring the services, not so much, but I think even more so like having some sort of hybrid like offering where like maybe we're just doing the maintenance and your team's doing the creation in our platform. Like there's a lot of options like that where it's like if you need your team to just quickly jump on and creating a test case, you could log into our platform and do that and then tell us, hey, we created this test case. Can you guys maintain that for us and execute it for us on a daily cadence like that's something that's also like I think worth exploring in the future, but, yeah.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: I was curious what's onboarding offboarding like if we do have some playwright tests, do those just transfer over? And then we just have to have the playwright ones rewritten or is this more proprietary kind of an API setup and we have to rewrite them for this? I'm gonna ask.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Ian, have you dealt with any migration from playwright to our platform? I know we migrate out to you. So you own all the test cases that we create in our platform. I just don't know like Ian, have you personally done any migration? Yeah, it's.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.text: pretty simple because it's all playwright. We have a couple proprietary things that we do in our platform. Like the launch code is a proprietary helper that translates directly into vanilla playwright. And we also have a proprietary inboxing capabilities through our inbox that we have set up in our platform. So that's another area where we would have to transfer some stuff over. And then all of the code that we build gets transferred out as a playwright project. So it's just vanilla playwright. And then again, the inboxing capabilities you might not be able to have. So that's something that would have to be set up by an engineer on your side if you were to want to export the code. But all of the code that we build and maintain is yours to keep at the end of an engagement. And then we give that to you. But it's all vanilla playwright and pretty easy to stand up in house if that's something that you were looking for. Yeah, I.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: think another part to answer your question fully is that we could migrate the cypress tests into our platform. I'm sure that would come at a cost and then the playwright ones I think would be easier if you're driving the creation, then I would assume that we would help and just give you pointers on the migration and it probably wouldn't be too difficult but you would then be able to upload those playwright scripts into our playwright platform and playwright based platform and then have it all kind of organized in one place. Yeah, where?

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: Are you guys using AI most in your platform? Yeah, no great.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Question. So, it's two parts. One is the creation just like we'll show you the demo in a second, but it's basically.

transcript.speaker: Jamie
transcript.text: Prompt based?

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: And it's going to be creating script as well. So like a lot of times it's either like creating script or it's creating like object recognition, like in the back end, ours is creating script in playwright, and then it's going to be for the creation side. And then it's also going to be for the scoping side like test planning side, exploratory side. So those are the two main areas. I don't know if you want to just jump into the demo now, Ian, it might just make sense.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.text: I mean, we have a decent amount of time, but everything that Steven was talking about, I was planning just on showing in the platform whenever you wanted to cool. So like, yeah, you've pretty much just been giving an overview and it's all been a really good explanation, so, great job there. But yeah, I can get into the demo if that's what you'd like, yeah, I.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: Guess, do you have any additional questions, Jonathan right now? I'm sure I'll have.

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: thousands, but let's jump into the demo and we'll see what we already covered. So I don't have to double tag.

transcript.speaker: Stephen
transcript.text: That's what I was thinking. One thing just while you're pulling it up. Ian is just for you Jonathan. I have a question is just how many people do help with the front end testing? I know that there's like 160 engineers total at the company. Is it like 50 people that do testing closer?

transcript.speaker: Jonathan
transcript.text: To 35?

transcript.speaker: Jamie
transcript.text: Can you guys see this? Yeah.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.text: Cool. So, this here is the QA wolf platform. This in particular is my AI sandbox that I use to do AI exploration and creation. So on the left side here, this is where the flows of your suite are going to live. So a flow we describe like an end to end test. It's like what the user is doing on the platform. So that's kind of how we denote a test and that's using our aaa model. So that's a range act and assert which is similar to gherkin, just more drawn out of a framework for end to end modeling. So, yeah, when we are building these flows, we'll plan them out here. And then we'll start implementing them. So I can, yeah… I'll build a flow using the AI just to show you our creation tool that's one of the tools that I use the most in terms of increasing my velocity as someone building these suites.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.text: And then on our platform, we also have two modes. So we have like a code mode which you'll be able to see all of the playwright code.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.text: We have access to JavaScript dependencies. So we can import things like faker, datefns to help with our flows. And then we also have the support of our AI to be able to import.

transcript.speaker: Jamie
transcript.text: Those.

transcript.speaker: Ian
